root_cause: Regular-expression denial of service (ReDoS) in regex-svc. A 'WAF rule' matches request input against the catastrophically-backtracking pattern ^(a+)+$ using a real backtracking engine (dlclark/regexp2) with no match timeout/complexity limit. The rule was active because WAF_RULE_DISABLED was unset. The sreft-cpu-load container continuously sent the pathological payload ('a' x26 + '!'), which forces exponential backtracking that pins the single CPU (container capped at cpus=1.0) for seconds per evaluation. Because /check and /healthz are served from the same fixed 4-worker pool, a handful of malicious requests held all 4 workers (inflight=4), so ordinary requests and the health probe could not acquire a worker and timed out. The process stayed 'up' but was unresponsive: /healthz took >3s and CPU sat at ~100%.
actions_taken: Investigated with docker ps / docker stats (found sreft-regex-svc pinned at ~100% CPU while neighbors idle), docker logs (saw 'WAF rule ENABLED (vulnerable)'), and the service's /debug endpoint (waf_rule_enabled=true, inflight=4, pattern=^(a+)+$). Confirmed the outage by probing /healthz (HTTP 200 but ~3.2s, exceeding health-check timeouts). Read app/main.go to confirm the ReDoS mechanism and the built-in kill switch. Remediated with the least-risky fix: edited docker-compose.yaml to set WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1 on regex-svc only, then recreated just that service with 'docker compose up -d --no-deps regex-svc'. Did NOT touch the unrelated payments neighbor or the load generator, and made no destructive changes. Verified recovery: /healthz now returns in ~3-6ms, /debug shows waf_rule_enabled=false and inflight=0, and regex-svc CPU fell from ~101% to ~2%. payments remained stable throughout.
postmortem: A vulnerable WAF regex (^(a+)+$) with unbounded backtracking and no evaluation timeout was evaluated inline within a fixed, small worker pool shared by the health endpoint. Crafted input triggered catastrophic backtracking that consumed every worker, starving health checks and legitimate traffic — the same failure class as the Cloudflare 2019 and Stack Overflow 2016 outages. Service was restored by activating the WAF kill switch (WAF_RULE_DISABLED=1), the analog of rolling back the bad rule, which frees the pool. This is blameless: the design coupled an untrusted, unbounded computation to the request-serving threads and lacked guardrails. Follow-ups: (1) add a per-evaluation regex match timeout / complexity budget; (2) replace or rewrite the pattern to avoid nested quantifiers, or use a linear-time RE2-based engine; (3) isolate expensive checks from the health-check path (separate pool or a lightweight liveness probe that doesn't take a worker token); (4) add CPU/latency/inflight alerting and input-length limits; (5) keep the kill switch documented and one flag away for fast mitigation.
